findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant outer group and quantifier: `/^[a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+$/`. This matches the exact same set of strings in linear time (no nested repetition to backtrack over). If real email validation is intended, use a proper single-pass pattern with an `@` and domain, or a vetted library. Add a timing test over N=20..30 non-matching inputs to confirm the fix.
findings.summary: emailLikePattern = /^([a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+)+$/ (source.ts:3) wraps an inner `[a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+` in an outer `(...)+` over the identical character class — the classic exponential-backtracking pattern. On any input of N matching chars followed by a non-matching char that fails the `$` anchor (e.g. `"a".repeat(30) + "!"`), the engine tries ~2^N partitions, blocking the event loop. isEmailLike (source.ts:8-10) exposes this over 'user-supplied email-like strings' (per L2 comment), so attacker-controlled input reaches the vulnerable regex. The comment on L5-7 accurately documents this as a real bug; the code and stated intent agree. Currently 0 callers, which caps present impact, but both symbols are exported as a reusable validator.
findings.title: ReDoS: nested quantifier in emailLikePattern causes catastrophic backtracking

summary:
Risk-tiered review (trivial) completed.

Single-file change introduces a genuine ReDoS vulnerability. The regex /^([a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+)+$/ on line 3 has a nested quantifier over an overlapping character class, producing exponential backtracking on non-matching inputs — a real event-loop DoS. Assumption-integrity check: the code and its comments are consistent (the comment correctly labels the defect), so there is no misleading premise for the next agent; the issue is that the documented bug is live in exported API surface (isEmailLike). Blast radius is currently 0 callers, so present-day exploitability is nil, but the symbols are exported for user-input validation. Fix is a one-line regex change to a linear-time equivalent.

Single 10-line new file introducing an exported email-like validator whose regex /^([a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+)+$/ is a textbook catastrophic-backtracking (ReDoS) pattern. The defect is real and self-documented in the code comment. It currently has 0 callers so it is not yet exploitable in-process, but it is clearly intended for user-supplied input (line 2), making it a high-severity latent security hole. Fix is a one-character-class change to remove the nested quantifier.
